summary: Gruber documents the flood of shameless Wordle rip-offs that appeared in Apple's App Store, copying not just the game mechanics but the exact name and design of Josh Wardle's free, ad-free web game. He catalogs several offenders, reserving special contempt for Zach Shakked's 'Wordle - The App,' which charged $30/year for a Pro unlock and whose creator bragged publicly about skyrocketing downloads. The indie developer community swiftly called out Shakked, who then privatized his Twitter account — but not before an old tweet surfaced in which he railed against copycats. Apple eventually removed the rip-offs, but Gruber's implicit criticism is that Apple allowed them in the first place.
key_points: Apple's App Store was flooded with apps copying Wordle's name, design, and mechanics, with multiple clones ranking among the top word games
Zach Shakked's 'Wordle - The App' was the most egregious: stealing the name, charging $30/year, and bragging about revenue before being publicly shamed
Shakked's own prior tweet condemning copycats resurfaced, exposing his hypocrisy in spectacular fashion
Apple initially did nothing about the rip-offs, only removing them after public backlash — raising questions about App Store curation
The original Wordle is free, ad-free, and works perfectly as a web app on iOS via Safari's Add to Home Screen
money_quotes: Apple, apparently, is just fine with this.
And then we get to the real gem of the bunch. 'Wordle - The App', by Zach Shakked, a free-to-download app with a 30-fucking-dollar-per-year 'Pro' unlock.
We're going to the fucking moon.
Within moments, Shakked took his Twitter account (@zachshakked) private. (He left his rip-off app in the App Store, of course, so as not to interrupt its onward trajectory to the moon.)
I absolutely despise copycats. Shameless copying is so dumb.
Shakked was wrong about that: shamelessly ripping off Wordle has gotten him somewhere, all right.
Wordle, it's worth noting, has no ads and is free of charge.
The actual official Wordle game — on the web — works splendidly as an app on iOS with Safari's 'Add to Home Screen' command in the share sheet.
key_insight: Apple's App Store curation fails to protect creators from brazen copycats, and the resulting gold rush exposes both platform negligence and developer shamelessness.
